title: Decoupling, exponential sums and the Riemann zeta function
partial_reason: Only 3 formal math environments parsed (thm:1, thm:2, cor:3); the headline results — the exponential-sum bound (§3, eq. 3.19), the $|\zeta(1/2+it)|\ll t^{13/84+\epsilon}$ bound (§4), and the exponent-pair statement (§5, 'Theorem 6') — live in running text and are represented here by their section nodes.

thm:1.summary: A multilinear ($d/2$-linear) decoupling inequality for the curve $\Phi$: for $O(1)$-separated subintervals $I_1,\dots,I_{d/2}\subset[0,1]$ and the partition $\{I_\tau\}$ of $[0,1]$ into $N^{-1/2}$-intervals, $\|\prod_j|\int_{I_j}a_j(t)e(x\cdot\Phi(t))dt|^{2/d}\|_{L^{3d}_\#(B_N)}\ll N^{1/6+\epsilon}\prod_j[\sum_{I_\tau\subset I_j}\|\int_{I_\tau}a_je(x\cdot\Phi)\|_{L^6_\#}^6]^{1/(3d)}$. The decoupling input, an adaptation of the Bourgain–Demeter method to this specific curve.

thm:2.summary: Mean value theorem: $\int_{[0,1]^2\times[-1,1]^2}|\sum_{n\le N}e(nx_1+n^2x_2+N^{1/2}n^{3/2}x_3+N^{1/2}n^{1/2}x_4)|^{12}dx\ll N^{6+\epsilon}$. The sixth-moment bound for the relevant exponential sum, deduced from the decoupling inequality thm:1.

cor:3.summary: Rescaled mean value: for $N^{-2}\le\delta\le1$, $N^{-1}\le\Delta\le1$, $A_6(N,\delta,\Delta)=\int|\sum_{n\le N}e(nx_1+n^2x_2+\tfrac1\delta(n/N)^{3/2}x_3+\tfrac1\Delta(n/N)^{1/2}x_4)|^{12}dx\ll\delta\Delta N^{9+\epsilon}$. The flexible form fed into the exponential-sum analysis.

sec:3.summary: Exponential-sum bound: for $S=\sum_{m\sim M}e(f(m))$ with $f(u)=TF(u/M)$, $F$ smooth on $[1/2,1]$ with $\min\{|F''|,|F'''|,|F''''|\}>c$ and $M\le\sqrt T$, a van der Corput-type process driven by the mean value theorem yields the new estimate (eq. 3.19) for $|S|$. The bridge from the moment bound to a pointwise exponential-sum bound.

sec:4.summary: Bounding $\zeta$ on the critical line: combining the §3 exponential-sum estimate (for the missing range $\alpha=\log M/\log T\in(13/42,17/42)$) with Huxley's bound and exponent-pair theory gives $|\zeta(1/2+it)|\ll t^{13/84+\epsilon}$ — the headline subconvexity bound, improving Huxley's $32/205$ exponent.

sec:5.summary: Further comments: the method shows $(13/84+\epsilon,\,55/84+\epsilon)$ is an exponent pair (the paper's 'Theorem 6'), valid under hypotheses weaker than a direct application of §3 would require ($M$ may exceed $\sqrt T$; summation over a sub-interval). The exponent-pair upgrade with downstream uses across analytic number theory.

edges.note: The sixth-moment / mean value bound is obtained by applying the decoupling inequality to the exponential sum and summing over the $N^{-1/2}$-pieces.
The rescaled mean value is the same decoupling estimate after an affine change of variables in the curve parameters.
Specializes/rescales the unparametrized mean value theorem.
The van der Corput iteration that bounds $|S|$ feeds on the rescaled mean value estimate at each scale.
The critical-line bound for $\zeta$ uses the new exponential-sum estimate to cover the previously inaccessible range of $\alpha$.
The exponent-pair statement is a re-packaging of the §3 exponential-sum bound under relaxed hypotheses.

decoupling_input.description: A multilinear decoupling inequality for the relevant curve, adapted from the Bourgain–Demeter method.

mean_value.description: Decoupling converted into a sixth-moment / mean value theorem for the exponential sum, in unparametrized and rescaled forms.

exponential_sums_and_zeta.description: A van der Corput-type process turns the moment bound into a pointwise exponential-sum estimate, which yields the $t^{13/84+\epsilon}$ bound on $|\zeta(1/2+it)|$ and a new exponent pair.

BD.title: The proof of the l^2 Decoupling Conjecture
